content:
initial research and setup for the project.

we are going to create a plugin for Obsidian which turns its editor into a true outliner experience. by true outliner I mean the functionality and UX similar to 

* Logseq (opensource, almost perfect, but stuck for years in a major rewrite)
* outl (new, opensource, similar to Logseq, very promising)
* Tana Outliner (recently split up into a separate app)
* Workflowy
* Roam research (well-known)
* Orca note (mentions bi-directional outlining, [https://orca-studio.com/orcanote](https://orca-studio.com/orcanote/))
* see the general info: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outliner
* do your own research for more concrete details about the UI, the functionality and the mechanics of a good outliner app.

---

after doing the research and getting a good grasp of what the general idea is, let's look at the existing Obsidian plugins:

* https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/obsidian-outliner
   * a good improvement to the default Obsidian experience, improved the writing with nested lists a lot, 
   * but it's still a flat text under the hood, with some cursor tricks bolted on top
      * selecting nodes reveals that very clearly: you can't select node-per-node, only the raw text behind, together with uneven indentation, etc.
         * a clumsy select and delete will leave a broken outline tree with messed up structure and indentation
      * it's still possible to just add a newline and start writing normal flat markdown, next to a list/outline
   * it doesn't "enforce" the structure in any way
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/bullet
      * a fork of the outliner plugin
* https://github.com/vslinko/obsidian-zoom
   * common outline feature, to zoom into a subtree
   * not essential for me, but a nice to have
* https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/draggable-list-items
   * this functionality is probably included in the outliner plugin
* bi-directionality of outline: backlinks view can benefit a lot from relying on the structure of the quoting documents (this is really good in Logseq)
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/better-search-views (not outliner-specific, but tries to improve the structure display in the search/backilns views)
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/influx (a direct attempt, but in a stale/abandoned state)
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/coalesce (a newer attempt at better backlinks display)

it's important not to mix up the Outliner concept with the "sidebar outline" in Obsidian (or any other editor) which shows a TOC/minimam structure of a document. they are related in a way, but we are focusing on the document's structure itself and the editing experience, not an extra UI on a side.

---

next, I want you to check my experiment with a TUI outliner editor: `tui-outliner-multiline`. it's a raw prototype and isn't a reference of the desired implementation, but there might be parts of it that are valuable. in particular, I want you to inspect the `specs/` folder (core-outliner, multiline edits), the specs and the user scenario descriptions. that project was built using spec-driven development (with SpecKit) and we are going to use SDD here as well (but with OpenSpec tooling). take into account that this is just an inspiration and some of the described scenarios/behaviors in that project are biased towards the TUI-specifics. 

---

I want you to conduct a broad and deep research, document thoroughly the understanding of what the goal is, the examples of good/bad, the known issues, the opportunities, etc. etc. anything that comes up as relevant.

the goal is to create a true outliner experience in Obsidian in a form of a comprehensive coherent plugin with a perfect scorecard (security, guidelines, no private APIs or hacks, etc.). this is an additional point of research: is it possible to achieve the desired experience with the existing plugin APIs in Obsdian?

---

if anything is unclear, or questions/doubts arise during/after the research, don't make any assumptions, stop and ask me directly. there is some space for interpretation and ambiguity, different preferences, etc. so I want to make sure we have a full alignment before committing to any plan.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## c9886e0d-2fc9-452f-a984-23dcb04932ed
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_automation_manage: Manage Devin automations — list, get, create, update, delete, or fetch the trigger event schemas (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- openspec-apply-change: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change. Use when the user wants to start implementing, continue implementation, or work through tasks.
- openspec-archive-change: Archive a completed change in the experimental workflow. Use when the user wants to finalize and archive a change after implementation is complete.
- openspec-explore: Enter explore mode - a thinking partner for exploring ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements. Use when the user wants to think through something before or during a change.
- openspec-propose: Propose a new change with all artifacts generated in one step. Use when the user wants to quickly describe what they want to build and get a complete proposal with design, specs, and tasks ready for implementation.
- openspec-sync-specs: Sync delta specs from a change to main specs. Use when the user wants to update main specs with changes from a delta spec, without archiving the change.
- openspec-update-change: Update an OpenSpec change by revising its existing planning artifacts and keeping them coherent with one another. Use when the user wants to revise a change's plan, fold new decisions into it, or reconcile its artifacts after an edit. Never edits code.
- opsx:apply: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change (Experimental)
- opsx:archive: Archive a completed change in the experimental workflow
- opsx:explore: Enter explore mode - think through ideas, investigate problems, clarify requirements
- opsx:propose: Propose a new change - create it and generate all artifacts in one step
- opsx:sync: Sync delta specs from a change to main specs
- opsx:update: Update a change - revise existing planning artifacts and keep them coherent (Experimental)
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mct-cbt-therapy: AI-assisted therapy integrating Metacognitive Therapy (MCT) and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT). Use when conducting therapeutic conversations, providing mental health support, or when user seeks help with anxiety, depression, worry, rumination, negative thought patterns, or emotional distress. Triggers on therapy-related requests, mental health discussions, or explicit requests for MCT/CBT techniques.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:phenomenology-guide: Comparative contemplative philosophy guide for understanding experiences in meditation and daily life. Use when user asks "what is this state?", "why does X happen in practice?", "what did tradition Y believe about Z?", wants to understand contemplative phenomena, seeks connections between traditions, or wants to learn history of philosophy, theology, and traditional metaphysics alongside naturalized interpretations. Maps experiences to both scientific mechanisms and traditional frameworks without forcing metaphysical commitment.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:practice-architecture: Design and refine personal contemplative practice systems. Use when user wants to build a practice routine, asks "what should I practice?", needs help sequencing practices, feels scattered across techniques, wants to develop consistency, struggles with habit discipline, or asks about integrating different traditions. Provides structure while respecting individual constraints and preferences. Emphasizes minimal viable practice over ambitious programs that collapse.
- anthropic-skills:reactive-pattern: Multi-layer intervention for intense reactive patterns—rumination loops, anxiety spirals, emotional flooding, obsessive thought trains. Use when user is caught in a strong pattern that standard techniques haven't touched, when they're spinning in their head, when emotions are overwhelming, or when they need in-the-moment guidance beyond thought records. Integrates somatic state assessment, nervous system regulation, feeling-tone awareness (vedanā), Stoic impression testing, and MCT detached mindfulness.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:somatic-navigator: Body-based nervous system regulation and interoceptive awareness guide. Use when user needs to regulate their physiological state, wants to understand their nervous system, is too activated for cognitive approaches, experiences anxiety/panic, feels shutdown/numb/frozen, asks about breathwork or body-based practices, or wants to develop body awareness. Provides specific protocols with timing and physiological rationale—neurobiology, not mysticism.
- anthropic-skills:values-clarity: Socratic dialogue for exploring what matters, clarifying personal values, and addressing values-action gaps. Use when user questions life direction, feels meaningless or stuck, experiences conflict between what they do and what matters to them, wants to understand their priorities, or asks about purpose, meaning, or "what should I do with my life." Integrates ACT values work, Stoic virtue ethics, and naturalized Buddhist ethics without metaphysical baggage.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# TUI Outliner
2	
3	[![Test](https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner/actions/workflows/test.yml)
4	[![codecov](https://codecov.io/github/laughedelic/tui-outliner/graph/badge.svg?token=E1BT6H0HJC)](https://codecov.io/github/laughedelic/tui-outliner)
5	
6	> [!WARNING]
7	> This project is in active development. It is an experiment and isn't meant for real-world use. 
8	
9	A simple outliner for terminal. This is an experiment in building terminal UIs with [OpenTUI](https://github.com/sst/opentui) and React, using spec-driven development (via [Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)).
10	
11	## Quick Start
12	
13	### Prerequisites
14	
15	- **Bun** >=1.2.0 ([install](https://bun.sh/docs/installation))
16	- **Zig** (latest stable) ([install](https://ziglang.org/learn/getting-started/)) - required for OpenTUI builds
17	
18	### Installation
19	
20	```bash
21	# Clone the repository
22	git clone https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner.git
23	cd tui-outliner
24	
25	# Install dependencies
26	bun install
27	
28	# Run the application
29	bun run dev
30	```
31	
32	### Usage
33	
34	Open an existing outline:
35	
36	```bash
37	bun run dev path/to/outline.json
38	```
39	
40	Open an outline in read-only mode (prevents all edits):
41	
42	```bash
43	# Using long flag
44	bun run dev --read-only path/to/outline.json
45	
46	# Using short flag
47	bun run dev -r path/to/outline.json
48	```
49	
50	The `--read-only` (or `-r`) flag opens the outline in read-only mode for this session only. This is useful for browsing reference materials, documentation, or any hierarchical data without risk of accidental modifications. Read-only mode is not persisted to the file.
51	
52	## Keyboard Shortcuts
53	
54	### Navigation
55	
56	| Key       | Action                         |
57	| --------- | ------------------------------ |
58	| `↑` / `k` | Move to previous node          |
59	| `↓` / `j` | Move to next node              |
60	| `←` / `h` | Move to parent node            |
61	| `→` / `l` | Expand and move to first child |
62	| `Home`    | Jump to first node             |
63	| `End`     | Jump to last node              |
64	
65	### Editing
66	
67	| Key                       | Action                                                |
68	| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
69	| `i`                       | Edit at beginning of text (vim-style)                 |
70	| `a`                       | Edit at end of text (vim-style)                       |
71	| `Enter` (nav)             | Edit current node                                     |
72	| `Enter` (edit)            | Create sibling below                                  |
73	| `Shift+Enter` (edit)      | Insert newline (multiline text)                       |
74	| `o`                       | Create sibling below (or first child if has children) |
75	| `O`                       | Create sibling above                                  |
76	| `Backspace` (edit, empty) | Delete node and move to previous                      |
77	| `Backspace` (nav)         | Delete node (confirm if has children)                 |
78	| `Ctrl+D`                  | Force delete node with children                       |
79	| `Escape`                  | Exit edit mode                                        |
80	
81	### Structure
82	
83	| Key                     | Action                                       |
84	| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
85	| `Tab` / `Shift+→`       | Indent node (make child of previous sibling) |
86	| `Shift+Tab` / `Shift+←` | Dedent node (promote to parent's sibling)    |
87	| `Shift+J` / `Shift+↓`   | Move node down                               |
88	| `Shift+K` / `Shift+↑`   | Move node up                                 |
89	
90	### Visibility
91	
92	| Key     | Action             |
93	| ------- | ------------------ |
94	| `Space` | Toggle fold/unfold |
95	
96	### Zoom
97	
98	| Key               | Action                 |
99	| ----------------- | ---------------------- |
100	| `.`               | Zoom into focused node |
101	| `,`               | Zoom out to root       |
102	| `←` (on top node) | Zoom up one level      |
103	
104	### Undo/Redo
105	
106	| Key                       | Action                  |
107	| ------------------------- | ----------------------- |
108	| `Ctrl+Z` / `u`            | Undo last change        |
109	| `Ctrl+Shift+Z` / `Ctrl+R` | Redo last undone change |
110	
111	**Note**: Undo/redo supports all operations: text edits, node creation/deletion, moves, indent/dedent, fold/unfold, and zoom. History is limited to 50 steps and is cleared when loading a new file.
112	
113	### File Operations
114	
115	| Key                              | Action        |
116	| -------------------------------- | ------------- |
117	| `Ctrl+S`                         | Save manually |
118	| Auto-save after 2s of inactivity |
119	
120	## File Format
121	
122	Outlines are saved as JSON files with the following structure:
123	
124	```json
125	{
126	  "version": "1.0.0",
127	  "root": {
128	    "id": "root",
129	    "text": "",
130	    "children": [
131	      {
132	        "id": "uuid-1",
133	        "text": "First item",
134	        "children": [],
135	        "isFolded": false
136	      }
137	    ]
138	  }
139	}
140	```
141	
142	**Note**: Read-only mode is controlled via the `--read-only` CLI flag and is not persisted in the file format.
143	
144	### Backups
145	
146	Backups are automatically created as `.backup-TIMESTAMP.json` before each save.
147	
148	## Examples
149	
150	### Organizing Meeting Notes
151	
152	```
153	• Meeting Notes
154	  • Attendees
155	    • Alice
156	    • Bob
157	  • Discussion Points
158	    • Project Timeline
159	      • Phase 1: Complete
160	      • Phase 2: In Progress
161	    • Budget Review
162	  • Action Items
163	    • Follow up with client
164	    • Update documentation
165	```
166	
167	### Project Planning
168	
169	```
170	• Project Outline
171	  • Research Phase
172	    • Literature Review [folded]
173	    • Competitor Analysis [folded]
174	  • Development
175	    • MVP Features
176	      • User Authentication
177	      • Core Functionality
178	    • Testing
179	  • Launch
180	```
181	
182	### Read-Only Reference Documents
183	
184	Use the `--read-only` flag to view reference materials without accidentally editing them:
185	
186	```bash
187	# Open any file in read-only mode
188	bun run dev --read-only docs/project-spec.json
189	```
190	
191	Example read-only outline (navigation works, editing is blocked):
192	
193	```
194	• Project Specification [READ-ONLY]
195	  • Requirements
196	    • User must be able to navigate with arrow keys
197	    • Support for 1000+ nodes
198	  • Architecture
199	    • Frontend: React + OpenTUI
200	    • Backend: File-based storage
201	  • Constraints
202	    • Must work offline
203	    • No external dependencies
204	```
205	
206	### Multiline Text Nodes
207	
208	Use Shift+Enter to create nodes with multiline content:
209	
210	```
211	• Code Examples
212	  • Python Function
213	    function greet(name):
214	        """Greet a user by name"""
215	        return f"Hello, {name}!"
216	  • Meeting Notes
217	    Attendees: Alice, Bob, Charlie
218	
219	    Discussion:
220	    - Project timeline reviewed
221	    - Budget approved
222	
223	    Action items:
224	    - Follow up with stakeholders
225	    - Schedule next meeting
226	  • Documentation
227	    Installation:
228	    1. Install Bun
229	    2. Run: bun install
230	    3. Start: bun run dev
231	```
232	
233	## Development
234	
235	See [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for AI agent development instructions and [specs/001-core-outliner-mvp/quickstart.md](specs/001-core-outliner-mvp/quickstart.md) for detailed development guide.
236	
237	### Running Tests
238	
239	```bash
240	# Run all tests
241	bun test
242	
243	# Type checking
244	bun run typecheck
245	
246	# Manual testing
247	bun run dev
248	```
249	
250	### Project Structure
251	
252	```
253	tui-outliner/
254	├── src/
255	│   ├── components/     # React components
256	│   ├── hooks/         # Custom React hooks
257	│   ├── lib/           # Core logic (pure functions)
258	│   └── types/         # TypeScript types
259	├── tests/
260	│   ├── unit/          # Unit tests
261	│   ├── integration/   # Integration tests
262	│   └── fixtures/      # Test data
263	└── specs/             # Feature specifications
264	```
265	
266	## Credits
267	
268	Built with:
269	- [OpenTUI](https://github.com/sst/opentui) - Terminal UI framework
270	- [React](https://react.dev/) - UI library
271	- [Bun](https://bun.sh/) - JavaScript runtime
272	- [SpecKit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit) - Specification-driven development
273

# TUI Outliner

[![Test](https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner/actions/workflows/test.yml)
[![codecov](https://codecov.io/github/laughedelic/tui-outliner/graph/badge.svg?token=E1BT6H0HJC)](https://codecov.io/github/laughedelic/tui-outliner)

> [!WARNING]
> This project is in active development. It is an experiment and isn't meant for real-world use. 

A simple outliner for terminal. This is an experiment in building terminal UIs with [OpenTUI](https://github.com/sst/opentui) and React, using spec-driven development (via [Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)).

## Quick Start

### Prerequisites

- **Bun** >=1.2.0 ([install](https://bun.sh/docs/installation))
- **Zig** (latest stable) ([install](https://ziglang.org/learn/getting-started/)) - required for OpenTUI builds

### Installation

```bash
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner.git
cd tui-outliner

# Install dependencies
bun install

# Run the application
bun run dev
```

### Usage

Open an existing outline:

```bash
bun run dev path/to/outline.json
```

Open an outline in read-only mode (prevents all edits):

```bash
# Using long flag
bun run dev --read-only path/to/outline.json

# Using short flag
bun run dev -r path/to/outline.json
```

The `--read-only` (or `-r`) flag opens the outline in read-only mode for this session only. This is useful for browsing reference materials, documentation, or any hierarchical data without risk of accidental modifications. Read-only mode is not persisted to the file.

## Keyboard Shortcuts

### Navigation

| Key       | Action                         |
| --------- | ------------------------------ |
| `↑` / `k` | Move to previous node          |
| `↓` / `j` | Move to next node              |
| `←` / `h` | Move to parent node            |
| `→` / `l` | Expand and move to first child |
| `Home`    | Jump to first node             |
| `End`     | Jump to last node              |

### Editing

| Key                       | Action                                                |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `i`                       | Edit at beginning of text (vim-style)                 |
| `a`                       | Edit at end of text (vim-style)                       |
| `Enter` (nav)             | Edit current node                                     |
| `Enter` (edit)            | Create sibling below                                  |
| `Shift+Enter` (edit)      | Insert newline (multiline text)                       |
| `o`                       | Create sibling below (or first child if has children) |
| `O`                       | Create sibling above                                  |
| `Backspace` (edit, empty) | Delete node and move to previous                      |
| `Backspace` (nav)         | Delete node (confirm if has children)                 |
| `Ctrl+D`                  | Force delete node with children                       |
| `Escape`                  | Exit edit mode                                        |

### Structure

| Key                     | Action                                       |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| `Tab` / `Shift+→`       | Indent node (make child of previous sibling) |
| `Shift+Tab` / `Shift+←` | Dedent node (promote to parent's sibling)    |
| `Shift+J` / `Shift+↓`   | Move node down                               |
| `Shift+K` / `Shift+↑`   | Move node up                                 |

### Visibility

| Key     | Action             |
| ------- | ------------------ |
| `Space` | Toggle fold/unfold |

### Zoom

| Key               | Action                 |
| ----------------- | ---------------------- |
| `.`               | Zoom into focused node |
| `,`               | Zoom out to root       |
| `←` (on top node) | Zoom up one level      |

### Undo/Redo

| Key                       | Action                  |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| `Ctrl+Z` / `u`            | Undo last change        |
| `Ctrl+Shift+Z` / `Ctrl+R` | Redo last undone change |

**Note**: Undo/redo supports all operations: text edits, node creation/deletion, moves, indent/dedent, fold/unfold, and zoom. History is limited to 50 steps and is cleared when loading a new file.

### File Operations

| Key                              | Action        |
| -------------------------------- | ------------- |
| `Ctrl+S`                         | Save manually |
| Auto-save after 2s of inactivity |

## File Format

Outlines are saved as JSON files with the following structure:

```json
{
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "root": {
    "id": "root",
    "text": "",
    "children": [
      {
        "id": "uuid-1",
        "text": "First item",
        "children": [],
        "isFolded": false
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

**Note**: Read-only mode is controlled via the `--read-only` CLI flag and is not persisted in the file format.

### Backups

Backups are automatically created as `.backup-TIMESTAMP.json` before each save.

## Examples

### Organizing Meeting Notes

```
• Meeting Notes
  • Attendees
    • Alice
    • Bob
  • Discussion Points
    • Project Timeline
      • Phase 1: Complete
      • Phase 2: In Progress
    • Budget Review
  • Action Items
    • Follow up with client
    • Update documentation
```

### Project Planning

```
• Project Outline
  • Research Phase
    • Literature Review [folded]
    • Competitor Analysis [folded]
  • Development
    • MVP Features
      • User Authentication
      • Core Functionality
    • Testing
  • Launch
```

### Read-Only Reference Documents

Use the `--read-only` flag to view reference materials without accidentally editing them:

```bash
# Open any file in read-only mode
bun run dev --read-only docs/project-spec.json
```

Example read-only outline (navigation works, editing is blocked):

```
• Project Specification [READ-ONLY]
  • Requirements
    • User must be able to navigate with arrow keys
    • Support for 1000+ nodes
  • Architecture
    • Frontend: React + OpenTUI
    • Backend: File-based storage
  • Constraints
    • Must work offline
    • No external dependencies
```

### Multiline Text Nodes

Use Shift+Enter to create nodes with multiline content:

```
• Code Examples
  • Python Function
    function greet(name):
        """Greet a user by name"""
        return f"Hello, {name}!"
  • Meeting Notes
    Attendees: Alice, Bob, Charlie

    Discussion:
    - Project timeline reviewed
    - Budget approved

    Action items:
    - Follow up with stakeholders
    - Schedule next meeting
  • Documentation
    Installation:
    1. Install Bun
    2. Run: bun install
    3. Start: bun run dev
```

## Development

See [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for AI agent development instructions and [specs/001-core-outliner-mvp/quickstart.md](specs/001-core-outliner-mvp/quickstart.md) for detailed development guide.

### Running Tests

```bash
# Run all tests
bun test

# Type checking
bun run typecheck

# Manual testing
bun run dev
```

### Project Structure

```
tui-outliner/
├── src/
│   ├── components/     # React components
│   ├── hooks/         # Custom React hooks
│   ├── lib/           # Core logic (pure functions)
│   └── types/         # TypeScript types
├── tests/
│   ├── unit/          # Unit tests
│   ├── integration/   # Integration tests
│   └── fixtures/      # Test data
└── specs/             # Feature specifications
```

## Credits

Built with:
- [OpenTUI](https://github.com/sst/opentui) - Terminal UI framework
- [React](https://react.dev/) - UI library
- [Bun](https://bun.sh/) - JavaScript runtime
- [SpecKit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit) - Specification-driven development

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_automation_manage: Manage Devin automations — list, get, create, update, delete, or fetch the trigger event schemas (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)
