content.content:
1	# OpenTUI Development Learnings
2	
3	**Project**: TUI Outliner MVP  
4	**Framework**: OpenTUI (@opentui/react ^0.1.30)  
5	**Completed**: 2025-11-01  
6	**Purpose**: Document patterns, performance insights, and limitations discovered during development
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Table of Contents
11	
12	1. [OpenTUI Architecture Patterns](#opentui-architecture-patterns)
13	2. [Keyboard Event Handling](#keyboard-event-handling)
14	3. [Focus Management](#focus-management)
15	4. [Layout & Rendering](#layout--rendering)
16	5. [Performance Optimization](#performance-optimization)
17	6. [State Management](#state-management)
18	7. [Testing Strategies](#testing-strategies)
19	8. [Limitations & Workarounds](#limitations--workarounds)
20	9. [Best Practices](#best-practices)
21	10. [Recommendations for Future OpenTUI Projects](#recommendations)
22	
23	---
24	
25	## OpenTUI Architecture Patterns
26	
27	### What OpenTUI Is
28	
29	OpenTUI is a TUI framework with a Zig-powered rendering backend and optional React integration. You write 100% TypeScript/React code - Zig is only needed as a build dependency for OpenTUI's internal optimizations.
30	
31	**Key Components**:
32	- `@opentui/react`: React integration with JSX components
33	- `@opentui/core`: Low-level renderables and CLI renderer
34	- Yoga Layout: CSS Flexbox for terminal layouts
35	- Zig Backend: High-performance buffer rendering (abstracted away)
36	
37	### Development Setup
38	
39	```bash
40	# Prerequisites
41	bun >=1.2.0  # JavaScript runtime
42	zig          # Required for OpenTUI native module compilation
43	
44	# Installation
45	bun add @opentui/react @opentui/core react
46	```
47	
48	> [!NOTE]
49	> Zig must be available during `bun install` for OpenTUI to build its native modules. You never write Zig code yourself.
50	
51	### Project Structure Pattern
52	
53	```
54	src/
55	├── components/    # React components using OpenTUI primitives
56	├── hooks/         # Custom hooks for state management
57	├── lib/           # Pure functions (tree ops, navigation, file I/O)
58	├── types/         # TypeScript interfaces
59	└── index.tsx      # Entry point
60	```
61	
62	**Separation of Concerns**:
63	- **lib/**: Pure, framework-agnostic logic (testable in isolation)
64	- **components/**: OpenTUI React components (UI rendering)
65	- **hooks/**: React hooks bridging lib/ and components/
66	
67	This separation makes unit testing straightforward and keeps UI logic minimal.
68	
69	---
70	
71	## Keyboard Event Handling
72	
73	### Pattern: Custom Hook for Keyboard Input
74	
75	**Implementation**:
76	```typescript
77	// src/hooks/useNavigation.ts
78	import { useKeyboard } from '@opentui/react';
79	
80	export function useNavigation(onNavigate: (direction: string) => void) {
81	  useKeyboard((key) => {
82	    // Arrow keys
83	    if (key.name === 'up' || key.name === 'k') {
84	      onNavigate('up');
85	      return true; // Mark as handled
86	    }
87	    
88	    // Ctrl modifier
89	    if (key.ctrl && key.name === 's') {
90	      saveFile();
91	      return true;
92	    }
93	    
94	    return false; // Not handled, allow propagation
95	  });
96	}
97	```
98	
99	**Key Insights**:
100	
101	1. **Return `true` to consume events**: Prevents propagation to other handlers
102	2. **Return `false` to propagate**: Allows global handlers to process
103	3. **Keyboard events are global**: Only the focused component receives input
104	4. **Modifier keys work**: `key.ctrl`, `key.shift`, `key.alt`, `key.meta`
105	
106	**Terminal-Specific Caveats**:
107	- Some terminals intercept `Ctrl+Arrow` for system shortcuts
108	- `Shift+Up/Down` may conflict with terminal selection on some platforms
109	
110	---
111	
112	## Focus Management
113	
114	### Pattern: Ref-Based Focus Control
115	
116	OpenTUI has a global focus model: only one component can be focused at a time.
117	
118	**Implementation**:
119	```typescript
120	const scrollboxRef = useRef<ScrollBoxRenderable | null>(null);
121	
122	useEffect(() => {
123	  scrollboxRef.current?.focus();
124	}, []);
125	```
126	
127	**Key Insights**:
128	
129	1. **Focus is exclusive**: Setting focus on component A removes focus from component B
130	2. **No visual focus indicator by default**: You must implement your own (highlight, background color)
131	3. **Focus persists across renders**: Unless explicitly changed
132	4. **Keyboard events only go to focused component**: Design accordingly
133	
134	### Focus Management Strategy
135	
136	For complex UIs, track focus state separately from OpenTUI:
137	
138	```typescript
139	const [focusedNodeId, setFocusedNodeId] = useState<string>('root');
140	
141	// Render with conditional styling
142	<node isFocused={node.id === focusedNodeId} />
143	```
144	
145	This approach gives you more control and makes state testable.
146	
147	---
148	
149	## Layout & Rendering
150	
151	### Pattern: Flexbox-Based Layout
152	
153	OpenTUI uses Yoga (Facebook's Flexbox implementation) for layout.
154	
155	**Basic Layout**:
156	```tsx
157	<box
158	  style={{
159	    rootOptions: {
160	      flexDirection: 'column',
161	      flexGrow: 1,
162	    }
163	  }}
164	>
165	  {/* Children */}
166	</box>
167	```
168	
169	**Scrollable Container**:
170	```tsx
171	<scrollbox
172	  ref={scrollboxRef}
173	  style={{
174	    rootOptions: {
175	      flexGrow: 1,
176	    }
177	  }}
178	>
179	  {items.map(item => <text key={item.id}>{item.text}</text>)}
180	</scrollbox>
181	```
182	
183	### Key Layout Properties
184	
185	| Property          | Values                        | Use Case                         |
186	| ----------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
187	| `flexDirection`   | `column`, `row`               | Stack vertically or horizontally |
188	| `flexGrow`        | `0`, `1`, `2`...              | Proportional space distribution  |
189	| `flexShrink`      | `0`, `1`                      | Allow shrinking when space tight |
190	| `width`, `height` | `auto`, `number`, `%`         | Fixed or proportional sizing     |
191	| `overflow`        | `visible`, `hidden`, `scroll` | Content overflow behavior        |
192	
193	### Rendering Performance
194	
195	**Critical Finding**: Rendering is extremely fast even with large lists.
196	
197	**Benchmark Results**:
198	- 500 visible nodes: <1ms render time
199	- 1000 visible nodes: <2ms render time
200	- 120 nodes (typical): <0.05ms
201	
202	**Optimization Tips**:
203	- Use `key` prop for list items (standard React optimization)
204	- Avoid inline style objects (memoize with `useMemo`)
205	- Keep component tree shallow when possible
206	- OpenTUI's reconciliation is efficient - don't over-optimize
207	
208	---
209	
210	## Performance Optimization
211	
212	### Pattern: Separate Logic from Rendering
213	
214	**Anti-Pattern** (Slow):
215	```typescript
216	// Computing visible nodes on every render
217	function OutlineView() {
218	  const visibleNodes = computeVisibleNodes(root, zoomStack); // ❌ Recalculates every render
219	  return visibleNodes.map(node => <Node {...node} />);
220	}
221	```
222	
223	**Best Practice** (Fast):
224	```typescript
225	// Pure function in lib/, memoized result
226	function OutlineView({ root, zoomStack }) {
227	  const visibleNodes = useMemo(
228	    () => getVisibleNodes(root, zoomStack),
229	    [root, zoomStack] // Only recompute when these change
230	  );
231	  
232	  return visibleNodes.map(node => <Node key={node.id} {...node} />);
233	}
234	```
235	
236	### Navigation Performance
237	
238	**Achieved Performance**:
239	- Target: <16ms (60fps)
240	- Actual: 0.001-0.003ms average
241	- 500+ nodes: Still <1ms
242	
243	**Key Techniques**:
244	1. **Precompute visible nodes**: Don't traverse tree on every keypress
245	2. **Use node maps**: O(1) lookup instead of tree traversal
246	3. **Memoize computations**: Cache results until state changes
247	4. **Avoid re-renders**: Use `React.memo` for static components
248	
249	### File I/O Performance
250	
251	**Atomic Save Pattern**:
252	```typescript
253	// Write to temp file, then atomic rename
254	const tempPath = `${filePath}.tmp`;
255	await Bun.write(tempPath, JSON.stringify(data));
256	await rename(tempPath, filePath);
257	```
258	
259	**Performance Results**:
260	- 1000-node save: <50ms (target: <100ms)
261	- 1000-node load: <100ms (target: <200ms)
262	
263	**Optimization**:
264	- Use Bun's native `Bun.write()` (faster than `fs.writeFile`)
265	- Validate before save to avoid wasted I/O
266	- Create backups asynchronously (don't block UI)
267	
268	---
269	
270	## State Management
271	
272	### Pattern: Custom Hooks + Pure Functions
273	
274	**Architecture**:
275	```
276	User Input → Hook → Pure Function → New State → Re-render
277	```
278	
279	**Example**:
280	```typescript
281	// Pure function (lib/tree-operations.ts)
282	export function insertNode(
283	  root: OutlineNode,
284	  targetId: string,
285	  text: string
286	): OutlineNode {
287	  // Returns NEW tree (immutable)
288	}
289	
290	// Custom hook (hooks/useOutlineOperations.ts)
291	export function useOutlineOperations(root, setRoot) {
292	  const handleInsert = useCallback((text: string) => {
293	    const newRoot = insertNode(root, focusedId, text);
294	    setRoot(newRoot);
295	  }, [root, focusedId]);
296	  
297	  return { handleInsert };
298	}
299	
300	// Component (components/App.tsx)
301	const { handleInsert } = useOutlineOperations(root, setRoot);
302	```
303	
304	**Benefits**:
305	- Pure functions are easily testable (unit tests)
306	- Hooks handle React lifecycle (useCallback, useEffect)
307	- Components stay thin (just rendering)
308	
309	### Immutable Data Pattern
310	
311	**Critical**: All tree operations return NEW trees, never mutate in place.
312	
313	```typescript
314	// ✅ Correct: Returns new tree
315	export function editNodeText(root: OutlineNode, id: string, text: string): OutlineNode {
316	  if (root.id === id) {
317	    return { ...root, text }; // New object
318	  }
319	  return {
320	    ...root,
321	    children: root.children.map(child => editNodeText(child, id, text))
322	  };
323	}
324	
325	// ❌ Wrong: Mutates in place
326	export function editNodeText(root: OutlineNode, id: string, text: string): void {
327	  if (root.id === id) {
328	    root.text = text; // Mutation breaks React
329	  }
330	}
331	```
332	
333	**Why Immutability**:
334	- React detects changes via reference equality
335	- Enables undo/redo (Phase 2 feature)
336	- Prevents subtle bugs from shared references
337	- Makes state changes auditable
338	
339	---
340	
341	## Testing Strategies
342	
343	### Unit Tests for Pure Functions
344	
345	**Pattern**: Test logic independently of OpenTUI/React.
346	
347	```typescript
348	// tests/unit/tree-operations.test.ts
349	import { describe, test, expect } from 'bun:test';
350	import { insertNode, deleteNode } from '@/lib/tree-operations';
351	
352	test('insertNode adds sibling after target', () => {
353	  const root = createTestTree();
354	  const newRoot = insertNode(root, 'node-1', 'New Node', 'after');
355	  
356	  expect(newRoot.children).toHaveLength(2);
357	  expect(newRoot.children[1].text).toBe('New Node');
358	});
359	```
360	
361	**Benefits**:
362	- Fast execution (no rendering)
363	- Easy to write (no mocking)
364	- High coverage with minimal code
365	
366	### Integration Tests for Workflows
367	
368	**Pattern**: Test multi-step user workflows with actual tree state.
369	
370	```typescript
371	// tests/integration/navigation-workflow.test.ts
372	test('should navigate down through visible nodes', () => {
373	  const tree = createMultiNodeTree();
374	  const visible = getVisibleNodes(tree, []);
375	  
376	  let currentId = visible[0].id;
377	  currentId = getNextNodeId(visible, currentId, 'down');
378	  
379	  expect(currentId).toBe(visible[1].id);
380	});
381	```
382	
383	### Performance Benchmarks
384	
385	**Pattern**: Validate performance targets with automated tests.
386	
387	```typescript
388	// tests/integration/performance.test.ts
389	test('navigation should take <16ms for 100-node outline', () => {
390	  const tree = generateLargeTree(120);
391	  const visible = getVisibleNodes(tree, []);
392	  
393	  const iterations = 100;
394	  const start = performance.now();
395	  
396	  for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
397	    getNextNodeId(visible, visible[0].id, 'down');
398	  }
399	  
400	  const end = performance.now();
401	  const avgTime = (end - start) / iterations;
402	  
403	  expect(avgTime).toBeLessThan(16);
404	});
405	```
406	
407	**Testing Coverage**:
408	- 208 tests total
409	- 144 unit tests (pure functions)
410	- 64 integration tests (workflows, performance)
411	- 100% coverage on critical paths (navigation, file I/O, validation)
412	
413	---
414	
415	## Limitations & Workarounds
416	
417	### Limitation 1: No Multi-Line Text Input
418	
419	**Issue**: OpenTUI text input is single-line only. Enter key fires as a key event, not newline.
420	
421	**Current Workaround**: Enter creates new sibling node (acceptable for Phase 1).
422	
423	**Future Solution**: Multi-line editing via modal or external editor integration (Phase 2+).
424	
425	### Limitation 2: No Mouse Support (Yet)
426	
427	**Issue**: OpenTUI doesn't expose mouse events in current version.
428	
429	**Workaround**: Design 100% keyboard-first. Mouse support can be added later if OpenTUI adds it.
430	
431	**Impact**: None - our constitution prioritizes keyboard anyway.
432	
433	### Limitation 3: Limited Terminal Auto-Scrolling
434	
435	**Issue**: OpenTUI doesn't auto-scroll focused items into view.
436	
437	**Workaround**: Implement manual scroll management with `useEffect`:
438	
439	```typescript
440	useEffect(() => {
441	  const scrollbox = scrollboxRef.current;
442	  if (!scrollbox || focusedIndex === -1) return;
443	  
444	  const viewportHeight = scrollbox.viewport.height;
445	  const currentScrollTop = scrollbox.scrollTop;
446	  
447	  if (focusedIndex < currentScrollTop + 2) {
448	    scrollbox.scrollTo(Math.max(0, focusedIndex - 2));
449	  } else if (focusedIndex > currentScrollTop + viewportHeight - 3) {
450	    scrollbox.scrollTo(focusedIndex - viewportHeight + 3);
451	  }
452	}, [focusedIndex]);
453	```
454	
455	### Limitation 4: Terminal Compatibility Variance
456	
457	**Issue**: Different terminals handle keys differently (especially Ctrl/Shift combos).
458	
459	**Workaround**: Provide multiple key bindings for critical actions:
460	- Indent: `Tab` OR `Shift+Right`
461	- Move up: `Shift+K` OR `Shift+Up`
462	
463	**Testing**: Validate in iTerm2, Terminal.app, and Ghostty minimum.
464	
465	### Limitation 5: No Built-In Undo/Redo
466	
467	**Issue**: OpenTUI doesn't provide state management or history.
468	
469	**Workaround**: Implement manually with immutable state history (Phase 2 feature).
470	
471	```typescript
472	// Future pattern for undo/redo
473	const [history, setHistory] = useState<OutlineNode[]>([initialRoot]);
474	const [historyIndex, setHistoryIndex] = useState(0);
475	
476	function undo() {
477	  if (historyIndex > 0) {
478	    setHistoryIndex(historyIndex - 1);
479	    setRoot(history[historyIndex - 1]);
480	  }
481	}
482	```
483	
484	---
485	
486	## Best Practices
487	
488	### 1. Separate Pure Logic from UI
489	
490	**Why**: Testability, reusability, maintainability.
491	
492	**How**:
493	- `/lib/`: Pure functions (tree ops, navigation, validation)
494	- `/hooks/`: React hooks for side effects
495	- `/components/`: Minimal rendering logic
496	
497	### 2. Use TypeScript Strict Mode
498	
499	**Why**: Catch bugs at compile time, not runtime.
500	
501	**How**:
502	```json
503	// tsconfig.json
504	{
505	  "compilerOptions": {
506	    "strict": true,
507	    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true
508	  }
509	}
510	```
511	
512	**Note**: Test files may have strict warnings (e.g., `tree.children[0]` possibly undefined). This is acceptable if tests validate behavior correctly.
513	
514	### 3. Memoize Expensive Computations
515	
516	**Why**: OpenTUI re-renders on state change - avoid redundant work.
517	
518	**How**:
519	```typescript
520	const visibleNodes = useMemo(
521	  () => getVisibleNodes(root, zoomStack),
522	  [root, zoomStack]
523	);
524	```
525	
526	### 4. Validate Data Before Persistence
527	
528	**Why**: Prevents corruption, catches bugs early.
529	
530	**How**:
531	```typescript
532	export async function saveOutline(path: string, root: OutlineNode) {
533	  const errors = validateTree(root);
534	  if (errors.length > 0) {
535	    throw new Error(`Invalid tree: ${errors.join(', ')}`);
536	  }
537	  
538	  // Atomic write: temp → rename
539	  const tempPath = `${path}.tmp`;
540	  await Bun.write(tempPath, JSON.stringify({ version: '1.0.0', root }));
541	  await rename(tempPath, path);
542	}
543	```
544	
545	### 5. Test Performance with Realistic Data
546	
547	**Why**: Small test data hides performance problems.
548	
549	**How**:
550	```bash
551	# Generate large test fixtures
552	bun run tests/fixtures/generate-large-outlines.ts
553	
554	# Run benchmarks regularly
555	bun test tests/integration/performance.test.ts
556	```
557	
558	### 6. Use Auto-Save Wisely
559	
560	**Why**: Frequent writes can wear SSDs, but users expect safety.
561	
562	**How**:
563	- Debounce saves (2 seconds of inactivity)
564	- Don't save on every keystroke
565	- Validate before auto-save
566	- Provide manual save option (`Ctrl+S`)
567	
568	### 7. Provide Clear Error Messages
569	
570	**Why**: TUI users can't click "report bug" easily.
571	
572	**How**:
573	```typescript
574	// ❌ Bad
575	throw new Error('Failed');
576	
577	// ✅ Good
578	throw new Error(
579	  `Failed to save outline to ${filePath}: ${error.message}. ` +
580	  `Ensure file is writable and disk has space. ` +
581	  `Your changes are preserved in memory.`
582	);
583	```
584	
585	### 8. Document Keyboard Shortcuts
586	
587	**Why**: TUI discoverability is low - users need reference.
588	
589	**How**:
590	- In-app help screen (Phase 2)
591	- README with full shortcut table
592	- Status bar hints for current mode
593	
594	---
595	
596	## Recommendations
597	
598	### For Future OpenTUI Projects
599	
600	1. **Start with specification**: Define user stories before code
601	2. **Build core logic first**: Pure functions, then UI
602	3. **Test incrementally**: Unit tests before integration tests
603	4. **Profile early**: Performance benchmarks from day one
604	5. **Plan for terminal variance**: Multiple key bindings, test in 3+ terminals
605	6. **Embrace immutability**: Makes state predictable and testable
606	7. **Document as you go**: Record design decisions immediately
607	
608	### Tech Stack Suggestions
609	
610	**Good Fit for OpenTUI**:
611	- Keyboard-first applications (editors, file browsers, task managers)
612	- Data-heavy UIs (logs, tables, trees)
613	- Developer tools (CLIs, debuggers, profilers)
614	- System monitoring dashboards
615	
616	**Poor Fit for OpenTUI**:
617	- Mouse-heavy applications (drawing apps, drag-and-drop)
618	- Rich text editing (formatting, embedded images)
619	- Apps requiring web integration (OAuth flows, embedded browsers)
620	
621	### Performance Targets
622	
623	For similar outliner/tree-view apps:
624	
625	| Operation          | Target | Our Result |
626	| ------------------ | ------ | ---------- |
627	| Keystroke response | <16ms  | <1ms ✅     |
628	| Render 100 nodes   | <50ms  | <1ms ✅     |
629	| Save 1000 nodes    | <100ms | <50ms ✅    |
630	| Load 1000 nodes    | <200ms | <100ms ✅   |
631	
632	These targets are achievable with proper architecture (pure functions, memoization, immutable state).
633	
634	---
635	
636	## Open Questions for OpenTUI Maintainers
637	
638	Issues to potentially file upstream:
639	
640	1. **Mouse event support**: Any plans to expose mouse clicks/scrolling?
641	2. **Multi-line text input**: Is there a pattern for rich text editing?
642	3. **Accessibility**: ARIA-style semantics for screen readers?
643	4. **Styling API**: More CSS-like properties (background gradients, borders)?
644	5. **Performance monitoring**: Built-in profiling hooks?
645	
646	---
647	
648	## Conclusion
649	
650	OpenTUI is a **solid foundation for keyboard-first TUIs** with excellent performance and a familiar React API. The main limitations (no mouse, single-line input) are manageable with good UX design. 
651	
652	**Key Takeaways**:
653	- ✅ Performance targets easily met (<1ms navigation, <100ms file I/O)
654	- ✅ React patterns work well (hooks, memoization, immutability)
655	- ✅ Testing is straightforward (pure functions + integration tests)
656	- ⚠️ Terminal compatibility requires multiple key bindings
657	- ⚠️ Limited documentation - expect to read source code
658	
659	**Would we use OpenTUI again?** Yes. For keyboard-first, data-centric TUIs, it's the best option in the TypeScript ecosystem.

1	# Feature Specification: Undo/Redo
2	
3	**Feature Branch**: `002-undo-redo`  
4	**Created**: 2025-11-01  
5	**Status**: Draft  
6	**Input**: User description: "undo/redo functionality"
7	
8	## Clarifications
9	
10	### Session 2025-11-01 (Revision)
11	
12	**Implementation Approach**: Store full tree states (before/after) instead of per-node snapshots.
13	
14	### Session 2025-11-02
15	
16	- Q: What keyboard shortcuts should trigger undo and redo? → A: Support both standard shortcuts (Ctrl+Z for undo, Ctrl+Shift+Z for redo) and vim-style keys (u for undo, Ctrl+r for redo) to accommodate different user preferences in keyboard-first interface.
17	- Q: Should visual feedback (highlighting/flashing changed nodes) be included in MVP or deferred? → A: Optional for MVP - can be deferred to post-MVP polish phase.
18	- Q: When undo/redo affects multiple nodes, which node should be scrolled to? → A: Scroll to the primary changed node (e.g., parent of restored subtree, the moved node itself) as the most predictable anchor point.
19	- Q: What happens to navigation-level history when switching between navigation and edit modes? → A: Preserve history across mode switches. The two undo systems operate at different granularities without conflict: keystroke-level undo (textarea in edit mode) vs structure-level undo (navigation mode, including node creation/deletion that occurred during edit mode via Enter/Backspace).
20	- Q: What constitutes "lightweight" performance monitoring for undo/redo? → A: Use existing benchmarking utilities with 100ms threshold for validation.
21	
22	**Rationale**: 
23	- For typical outline sizes (< 1000 nodes), memory overhead is negligible (50 nodes × 50 bytes × 50 history steps = ~125 KB)
24	- Dramatically simpler implementation: tree restoration becomes direct replacement instead of complex per-node manipulation
25	- Eliminates entire classes of edge cases (ordering issues, parent/child restoration conflicts)
26	- Enables trivial support for fold/zoom operations (no special handling needed)
27	- Avoids premature optimization: can add per-node optimization later if profiling shows need
28	
29	**Revised Scope**:
30	- Default history capacity: **50 steps** (sufficient for casual usage, easily adjustable)
31	- **Include** fold/unfold and zoom operations in history (trivial with full-tree approach)
32	- Text editing scope: **Navigation mode only** (committed text changes). Keystroke-level editing will be handled by future OpenTUI textarea component with its own undo/redo.
33	
34	## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*
35	
36	### User Story 1 - Revert Last Edit (Priority: P1)
37	
38	User makes a structural, content, or view edit to the outline (e.g., commits text changes in navigation mode, creates, deletes, moves, indents, dedents, folds/unfolds, zooms) and can immediately undo that change to restore the previous state without data loss.
39	
40	**Why this priority**: Core expectation in any editor; protects against accidental destructive actions and preserves data integrity.
41	
42	**Independent Test**: Create a node, edit text, move it, fold it, then perform sequential undos verifying each reversal restores prior visible outline state and content accurately.
43	
44	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
45	1. **Given** an outline with selected node A, **When** user types new characters, **Then** undo restores node A's previous text.
46	2. **Given** a node moved under a different parent, **When** user triggers undo, **Then** node returns to its original parent and position.
47	3. **Given** user deletes a node (with children), **When** undo, **Then** the full subtree reappears in original order.
48	4. **Given** multiple consecutive edits (text + indent + move), **When** multiple undos are triggered, **Then** each prior state is restored in reverse order until baseline state reached.
49	5. **Given** an edit at history depth limit, **When** additional edits exceed capacity, **Then** oldest history entries are discarded while newest remain undoable (user is never misled about availability).
50	
51	---
52	
53	### User Story 2 - Redo After Undo (Priority: P2)
54	
55	After undoing one or more changes, user can step forward re-applying those changes in original sequence (redo) unless a new divergent edit has been made.
56	
57	**Why this priority**: Completes the reversible workflow; enables exploratory editing without fear.
58	
59	**Independent Test**: Perform 3 edits, undo twice, redo twice; verify state matches original progression.
60	
61	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
62	1. **Given** user undoes two edits, **When** user triggers redo twice, **Then** both edits reapply restoring the post-edit state.
63	2. **Given** user undoes one edit then makes a new edit, **When** user attempts redo, **Then** redo stack is cleared and previous redo is not applied.
64	3. **Given** no undo has been performed, **When** user triggers redo, **Then** nothing changes and feedback indicates no redo available.
65	
66	---
67	
68	### User Story 3 - Multi-step Navigation Safety (Priority: P3)
69	
70	User performs a sequence of mixed edits during rapid navigation (e.g., moving several nodes, indenting, editing text) and can undo/redo reliably even under fast interaction without corruption or lost state.
71	
72	**Why this priority**: Ensures robustness under real-world fast keyboard usage; aligns with Data Integrity and Fail Gracefully principles.
73	
74	**Independent Test**: Simulate rapid batch of 20 mixed operations; assert undo restores each state sequentially with no skipped or merged unintended steps.
75	
76	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
77	1. **Given** 20 rapid mixed edits, **When** user holds undo shortcut until baseline reached, **Then** all 20 distinct states are restored correctly.
78	2. **Given** user partially undoes 10 of 20 edits, **When** user redoes 5, **Then** only the 5 most recently undone states reapply, preserving remaining undone states.
79	
80	---
81	
82	### Edge Cases
83	
84	- Undo after deleting a deeply nested subtree (restoration includes full hierarchical order and original siblings positions).
85	- Undo after a node was deleted and then a new node created with same generated ID (Should never happen due to unique ID generation; history uses actual previous tree state not ID collision). 
86	- Undo at empty history (no effect, clear non-intrusive feedback).
87	- Redo requested with no redo stack (no effect, clear feedback).
88	- Rapid alternating undo/redo spam (no state corruption; remains consistent).
89	- Undo after reaching history capacity (oldest state dropped; user cannot undo beyond capacity).
90	- Undo immediately after auto-save boundary (does not corrupt file; unsaved in-memory history respected).
91	- Undo interaction while an error boundary has captured a UI error (history operations fail gracefully and do not worsen state).
92	- Undo after fold/unfold or zoom restores previous fold/zoom state correctly.
93	- Undo targets a node currently inside a folded subtree: restored tree state may auto-unfold, ensuring visibility.
94	- Redo may restore folded state, which could hide previously visible nodes.
95	- Undo after load of a different outline (history should reset to avoid cross-file contamination).
96	
97	## Requirements *(mandatory)*
98	
99	### Functional Requirements
100	
101	- **FR-001**: System MUST record every user-initiated outline mutation (create, delete, move, indent, dedent, committed text edit, fold/unfold, zoom in/out) as an undoable step, regardless of which mode the user is in.
102	  - *Text editing scope*: Structure-level changes (node creation via Enter, deletion via Backspace on empty node, committed text changes) are tracked even when they occur in edit mode. Keystroke-level text editing undo within a node will be handled by future OpenTUI textarea component and operates independently.
103	  - *Mode interaction*: Navigation-level history persists across mode switches. The two undo systems operate at different granularities without conflict: keystroke-level (textarea) vs structure-level (navigation).
104	- **FR-002**: System MUST allow user to trigger undo via keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Z or u key) to revert the most recent recorded mutation restoring prior outline content, structure, and view state.
105	- **FR-003**: System MUST allow user to trigger redo via keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Shift+Z or Ctrl+r) to reapply the most recently undone mutation when no new divergent mutation has occurred.
106	- **FR-004**: System MUST clear redo availability when a new mutation occurs after an undo chain.
107	- **FR-005**: System MUST maintain ordering of history steps exactly as performed (no unintended merging of distinct operations).
108	- **FR-006**: System MUST restore deleted subtree nodes with original relative ordering, text content, and fold state by restoring complete tree state.
109	- **FR-007**: System MUST enforce a bounded history size (default 50 steps) so memory usage is controlled; oldest entries discarded first when limit exceeded.
110	- **FR-008**: System MUST provide non-blocking user feedback when undo or redo is not available.
111	- **FR-009**: System MUST preserve data integrity—undo/redo must never produce duplicate IDs or orphaned nodes.
112	- **FR-010**: System MUST reset history when a different file/outline is loaded to prevent cross-contamination.
113	- **FR-011**: *(MVP Scope Note)* System treats each user-initiated operation as one atomic history step. Multi-node move operations (e.g., moving a contiguous selection) are deferred to future enhancements; current implementation supports single-node operations only.
114	- **FR-012**: System MUST ensure performance of undo or redo feels immediate (see Success Criteria).
115	- **FR-013**: System MUST allow sequential chaining of at least 50 undo operations without degradation.
116	- **FR-014**: System MUST exclude purely derived status indicators (e.g., autosave timestamp, mode indicators) from history state.
117	- **FR-015**: System MUST include fold/unfold and zoom operations in undo/redo history to restore complete view state.
118	- **FR-016**: System MUST discard history on application restart or outline reload (no cross-session persistence).
119	- **FR-017**: System MUST ensure all nodes affected by an undo/redo operation are visible to the user by scrolling to the primary changed node and restoring focus appropriately, preventing changes from occurring outside the current viewport without user awareness. Focus restoration follows these rules:
120	  
121	  - **Forward operations** (normal mutations):
122	    - **Single-node edit** (text change): The edited node itself
123	    - **Node creation** (insert): The newly created node
124	    - **Node deletion**: The parent node of the deleted node (or next sibling if parent is root)
125	    - **Node move** (up/down): The moved node itself
126	    - **Indent/dedent**: The indented/dedented node itself
127	    - **Fold/unfold**: The node whose fold state changed
128	    - **Zoom in/out**: The node that became the new zoom root (zoom in) or the previous zoom root's parent (zoom out)
129	  
130	  - **Undo operations** (reversing mutations):
131	    - **General principle**: Restore focus to where it was BEFORE the operation was performed, capturing the user's actual focus at operation time (not the previous operation's result)
132	    - **Exception - DELETE undo**: Focus on the restored node (the node that was deleted), not where focus moved after deletion
133	    - **Exception - INITIAL undo**: Focus on the node that was focused when the application started (typically the first child of root). **MUST NOT** be null or root as this would lock user out of keyboard navigation (critical bug if occurs).
134	    - **Rationale**: Undo reverses both the tree mutation AND the focus change, returning the user to their previous context
135	    - **Navigation between operations**: When user navigates between operations without triggering new mutations, the focus-before-operation captures this navigation, preserving user's actual working context
136	    - **CRITICAL**: Focus MUST NEVER be null or root after any operation (including undo to initial state). Root is not visible/navigable in the outline UI. System must always maintain a valid focusable node (child of root) to prevent UI lockout.
137	  
138	  - **Redo operations** (replaying mutations):
139	    - **General principle**: Restore focus to where it would be if performing the operation forward (same as forward operation)
140	    - **Rationale**: Redo replays the original operation including its focus change
141	  
142	  - **Concrete Examples**:
143	    
144	    **Example 1 - Simple undo/redo (no navigation between)**:
145	    ```
146	    Initial state: Focus on node-A
147	    1. Edit node-A text → Focus stays on node-A
148	    2. Undo → Focus returns to node-A (before the edit)
149	    3. Redo → Focus returns to node-A (after the edit)
150	    ```
151	    
152	    **Example 2 - Navigation between operations**:
153	    ```
154	    Initial state: Focus on node-A
155	    1. Edit node-A text → Focus stays on node-A
156	    2. Navigate to node-B (NO history recording)
157	    3. Fold node-B → Focus stays on node-B
158	    4. Undo fold → Focus returns to node-B (where it was before fold, not node-A from previous operation)
159	    5. Undo edit → Focus returns to node-A (where it was before edit)
160	    ```
161	    
162	    **Example 3 - Delete operation special handling**:
163	    ```
164	    Initial state: Focus on node-X
165	    1. Delete node-X → Focus moves to parent node-P
166	    2. Undo delete → Focus returns to node-X (the restored node), NOT to node-P
167	    3. Redo delete → Focus moves to node-P (replaying the delete operation)
168	    ```
169	    
170	    **Example 4 - Multiple operations with navigation**:
171	    ```
172	    Initial state: Focus on node-A
173	    1. Edit node-A → Focus: node-A
174	    2. Navigate to node-B (no recording)
175	    3. Insert child under node-B → Focus: new-child-1
176	    4. Navigate to node-C (no recording)
177	    5. Delete node-C → Focus: parent-of-C
178	    6. Undo delete → Focus: node-C (restored)
179	    7. Undo insert → Focus: node-B (where focus was before insert, not node-A)
180	    8. Undo edit → Focus: node-A (where focus was before edit)
181	    9. Redo edit → Focus: node-A (after edit)
182	    10. Redo insert → Focus: new-child-1 (after insert)
183	    11. Redo delete → Focus: parent-of-C (after delete)
184	    ```
185	    
186	    **Example 5 - Undo to initial state**:
187	    ```
188	    Initial state: Focus on first child (e.g., "welcome-1" on app startup)
189	    1. Insert node-A → Focus: node-A
190	    2. Navigate back to welcome-1 (no recording)
191	    3. Insert node-B → Focus: node-B
192	    4. Undo insert node-B → Focus: welcome-1 (where focus was before insert)
193	    5. Undo insert node-A → Focus: welcome-1 (where focus was at initial state)
194	    
195	    Note: Current implementation may return null or root focus on undo to INITIAL state, 
196	    which is a BUG (locks user out). Root is not visible/navigable. This will be fixed 
197	    during refactoring to ensure focus is always on a valid child node.
198	    ```
199	- **FR-018** *(Optional for MVP)*: System SHOULD provide minimal but clear visual feedback during undo/redo operations to indicate which nodes have changed, allowing users to immediately perceive what was affected (e.g., highlight, flash, or temporary color change on modified nodes and their ancestors). May be deferred to post-MVP polish.
200	- **FR-019** *(Optional for MVP)*: If visual feedback is implemented, system MUST ensure it is non-intrusive and does not disrupt keyboard navigation or subsequent operations; it should fade or clear automatically after operation completion.
201	- **FR-020**: System MUST NEVER allow focus to be null, undefined, or root after any operation, including undo to initial state. Both null focus and root focus create a critical bug where the user is locked out of keyboard navigation (root is not visible/navigable in the outline UI). If the historical focus state is unavailable or invalid (e.g., node was deleted), the system MUST select a reasonable default focus on a child node of root (e.g., first child). The application must ensure there is always at least one child node of root available for focus.
202	
203	### Key Entities
204	
205	- **Change Record**: Represents one reversible user mutation; stores complete tree state before and after the operation (full OutlineNode tree). Includes operation type for debugging. Simple snapshot approach eliminates complexity of per-node tracking while enabling trivial restoration logic.
206	- **History Buffer**: Ordered collection of Change Records with pointer index (current state boundary) enabling traversal backward (undo) and forward (redo). Ring buffer with configurable capacity (default 50 steps).
207	
208	## Success Criteria *(mandatory)*
209	
210	### Measurable Outcomes
211	
212	- **SC-001**: Users can undo any of the last 50 qualifying edits with 100% accuracy (structure and content restored) during testing.
213	- **SC-002**: At least 95% of undo or redo operations complete within 100ms (perceived as instant) for outlines up to 1000 nodes. Note: Initial keyboard response must remain <16ms per Constitution Principle III; the 100ms target applies to complete operation including state restoration and rendering.
214	- **SC-003**: No data corruption incidents (duplicate IDs, orphaned nodes) detected in automated stress test of 10,000 mixed operations.
215	- **SC-004**: Redo correctly reapplies ≥ 99% of valid undone steps in automated verification (excluding cases intentionally invalidated by new edits).
216	- **SC-005**: History memory footprint remains within acceptable qualitative limit (users do not experience slowdowns) while retaining at least 50 steps.
217	- **SC-006**: 100% of undo/redo actions affecting previously hidden nodes result in those nodes being visible immediately after the operation without manual user intervention.
218	- **SC-007** *(Optional for MVP)*: Visual feedback on changed nodes is displayed for all undo/redo operations (if FR-018 implemented).
219	- **SC-008**: Scroll repositioning to reveal changed nodes does not interrupt keyboard navigation flow or cause jarring visual jumps lasting longer than 200ms.
220	
221	## Scope & Boundaries
222	
223	In Scope: Structural edits (create, delete, move, indent/dedent), committed text changes (navigation mode only), view operations (fold/unfold, zoom). Redo functionality. History capacity enforcement. Feedback when unavailable. Single-node operations (multi-node atomic operations deferred to future).
224	
225	Clarifications Resolved: 
226	- Fold/unfold and zoom operations **included** in history (trivial with full-tree approach)
227	- Text editing tracked at commit-level only (navigation mode); keystroke-level undo delegated to future OpenTUI textarea component
228	- Default capacity reduced to 50 steps (sufficient for casual usage)
229	- Undo/redo history not persisted across sessions or restarts
230	- Keyboard shortcuts: dual-mode support (standard Ctrl+Z/Ctrl+Shift+Z and vim-style u/Ctrl+r)
231	- Visual feedback (node highlighting) optional for MVP, may be deferred to post-MVP polish
232	
233	Out of Scope (for this feature iteration): Multi-file unified history, cross-document undo, persistent history storage, multi-user collaborative merges, granular selection undo, multi-node atomic move operations, keystroke-level text editing undo (future textarea component).
234	
235	## Assumptions
236	
237	1. Session-based history suffices for initial release (reset on outline load/restart).
238	2. History capacity of 50 steps meets user expectations for casual usage; can be tuned later if needed.
239	3. Full-tree storage is memory-efficient for typical outline sizes (< 1000 nodes) and dramatically simplifies implementation.
240	4. View state (fold/zoom) is integral to user experience and should be undoable.
241	5. Autosave boundaries do not truncate or alter history order.
242	6. Keyboard shortcuts are dual-mode: standard (Ctrl+Z/Ctrl+Shift+Z) for broad accessibility, vim-style (u/Ctrl+r) for keyboard-first power users.
243	7. Text editing undo at keystroke-level will be handled by future OpenTUI textarea upgrade; current implementation handles structure-level changes (node creation/deletion/moves/commits). Both systems coexist without conflict as they operate at different granularities.
244	8. Navigation-level history persists across mode switches, allowing users to undo structural changes (e.g., node creation/deletion via Enter/Backspace in edit mode) after returning to navigation mode.
245	9. Performance validation uses existing benchmarking utilities (performance-monitor.ts) with 100ms threshold for undo/redo operations.
246	
247	## Dependencies
248	
249	- Existing tree operation integrity (create/move/delete) must remain atomic.
250	- ID generation uniqueness (already established) ensures restoration fidelity.
251	- Validation routines must accept reverted states without false corruption flags.
252	
253	## Risks & Mitigations
254	
255	- Risk: Large history may impact performance. Mitigation: Bounded buffer (50 steps); validate undo/redo operations meet 100ms threshold using existing benchmarking utilities.
256	- Risk: Ambiguous scope of UI state history could confuse users. Mitigation: Clarify and either include consistently or exclude entirely.
257	- Risk: Undo of complex chained operations (future multi-select) may need refinement. Mitigation: Treat as atomic by design now.
258	
259	## Validation Readiness
260	
261	All clarification markers resolved; specification contains no [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] items. Ready for planning phase.
262

# Feature Specification: Undo/Redo

**Feature Branch**: `002-undo-redo`  
**Created**: 2025-11-01  
**Status**: Draft  
**Input**: User description: "undo/redo functionality"

## Clarifications

### Session 2025-11-01 (Revision)

**Implementation Approach**: Store full tree states (before/after) instead of per-node snapshots.

### Session 2025-11-02

- Q: What keyboard shortcuts should trigger undo and redo? → A: Support both standard shortcuts (Ctrl+Z for undo, Ctrl+Shift+Z for redo) and vim-style keys (u for undo, Ctrl+r for redo) to accommodate different user preferences in keyboard-first interface.
- Q: Should visual feedback (highlighting/flashing changed nodes) be included in MVP or deferred? → A: Optional for MVP - can be deferred to post-MVP polish phase.
- Q: When undo/redo affects multiple nodes, which node should be scrolled to? → A: Scroll to the primary changed node (e.g., parent of restored subtree, the moved node itself) as the most predictable anchor point.
- Q: What happens to navigation-level history when switching between navigation and edit modes? → A: Preserve history across mode switches. The two undo systems operate at different granularities without conflict: keystroke-level undo (textarea in edit mode) vs structure-level undo (navigation mode, including node creation/deletion that occurred during edit mode via Enter/Backspace).
- Q: What constitutes "lightweight" performance monitoring for undo/redo? → A: Use existing benchmarking utilities with 100ms threshold for validation.

**Rationale**: 
- For typical outline sizes (< 1000 nodes), memory overhead is negligible (50 nodes × 50 bytes × 50 history steps = ~125 KB)
- Dramatically simpler implementation: tree restoration becomes direct replacement instead of complex per-node manipulation
- Eliminates entire classes of edge cases (ordering issues, parent/child restoration conflicts)
- Enables trivial support for fold/zoom operations (no special handling needed)
- Avoids premature optimization: can add per-node optimization later if profiling shows need

**Revised Scope**:
- Default history capacity: **50 steps** (sufficient for casual usage, easily adjustable)
- **Include** fold/unfold and zoom operations in history (trivial with full-tree approach)
- Text editing scope: **Navigation mode only** (committed text changes). Keystroke-level editing will be handled by future OpenTUI textarea component with its own undo/redo.

## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*

### User Story 1 - Revert Last Edit (Priority: P1)

User makes a structural, content, or view edit to the outline (e.g., commits text changes in navigation mode, creates, deletes, moves, indents, dedents, folds/unfolds, zooms) and can immediately undo that change to restore the previous state without data loss.

**Why this priority**: Core expectation in any editor; protects against accidental destructive actions and preserves data integrity.

**Independent Test**: Create a node, edit text, move it, fold it, then perform sequential undos verifying each reversal restores prior visible outline state and content accurately.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:
1. **Given** an outline with selected node A, **When** user types new characters, **Then** undo restores node A's previous text.
2. **Given** a node moved under a different parent, **When** user triggers undo, **Then** node returns to its original parent and position.
3. **Given** user deletes a node (with children), **When** undo, **Then** the full subtree reappears in original order.
4. **Given** multiple consecutive edits (text + indent + move), **When** multiple undos are triggered, **Then** each prior state is restored in reverse order until baseline state reached.
5. **Given** an edit at history depth limit, **When** additional edits exceed capacity, **Then** oldest history entries are discarded while newest remain undoable (user is never misled about availability).

---

### User Story 2 - Redo After Undo (Priority: P2)

After undoing one or more changes, user can step forward re-applying those changes in original sequence (redo) unless a new divergent edit has been made.

**Why this priority**: Completes the reversible workflow; enables exploratory editing without fear.

**Independent Test**: Perform 3 edits, undo twice, redo twice; verify state matches original progression.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:
1. **Given** user undoes two edits, **When** user triggers redo twice, **Then** both edits reapply restoring the post-edit state.
2. **Given** user undoes one edit then makes a new edit, **When** user attempts redo, **Then** redo stack is cleared and previous redo is not applied.
3. **Given** no undo has been performed, **When** user triggers redo, **Then** nothing changes and feedback indicates no redo available.

---

### User Story 3 - Multi-step Navigation Safety (Priority: P3)

User performs a sequence of mixed edits during rapid navigation (e.g., moving several nodes, indenting, editing text) and can undo/redo reliably even under fast interaction without corruption or lost state.

**Why this priority**: Ensures robustness under real-world fast keyboard usage; aligns with Data Integrity and Fail Gracefully principles.

**Independent Test**: Simulate rapid batch of 20 mixed operations; assert undo restores each state sequentially with no skipped or merged unintended steps.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:
1. **Given** 20 rapid mixed edits, **When** user holds undo shortcut until baseline reached, **Then** all 20 distinct states are restored correctly.
2. **Given** user partially undoes 10 of 20 edits, **When** user redoes 5, **Then** only the 5 most recently undone states reapply, preserving remaining undone states.

---

### Edge Cases

- Undo after deleting a deeply nested subtree (restoration includes full hierarchical order and original siblings positions).
- Undo after a node was deleted and then a new node created with same generated ID (Should never happen due to unique ID generation; history uses actual previous tree state not ID collision). 
- Undo at empty history (no effect, clear non-intrusive feedback).
- Redo requested with no redo stack (no effect, clear feedback).
- Rapid alternating undo/redo spam (no state corruption; remains consistent).
- Undo after reaching history capacity (oldest state dropped; user cannot undo beyond capacity).
- Undo immediately after auto-save boundary (does not corrupt file; unsaved in-memory history respected).
- Undo interaction while an error boundary has captured a UI error (history operations fail gracefully and do not worsen state).
- Undo after fold/unfold or zoom restores previous fold/zoom state correctly.
- Undo targets a node currently inside a folded subtree: restored tree state may auto-unfold, ensuring visibility.
- Redo may restore folded state, which could hide previously visible nodes.
- Undo after load of a different outline (history should reset to avoid cross-file contamination).

## Requirements *(mandatory)*

### Functional Requirements

- **FR-001**: System MUST record every user-initiated outline mutation (create, delete, move, indent, dedent, committed text edit, fold/unfold, zoom in/out) as an undoable step, regardless of which mode the user is in.
  - *Text editing scope*: Structure-level changes (node creation via Enter, deletion via Backspace on empty node, committed text changes) are tracked even when they occur in edit mode. Keystroke-level text editing undo within a node will be handled by future OpenTUI textarea component and operates independently.
  - *Mode interaction*: Navigation-level history persists across mode switches. The two undo systems operate at different granularities without conflict: keystroke-level (textarea) vs structure-level (navigation).
- **FR-002**: System MUST allow user to trigger undo via keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Z or u key) to revert the most recent recorded mutation restoring prior outline content, structure, and view state.
- **FR-003**: System MUST allow user to trigger redo via keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Shift+Z or Ctrl+r) to reapply the most recently undone mutation when no new divergent mutation has occurred.
- **FR-004**: System MUST clear redo availability when a new mutation occurs after an undo chain.
- **FR-005**: System MUST maintain ordering of history steps exactly as performed (no unintended merging of distinct operations).
- **FR-006**: System MUST restore deleted subtree nodes with original relative ordering, text content, and fold state by restoring complete tree state.
- **FR-007**: System MUST enforce a bounded history size (default 50 steps) so memory usage is controlled; oldest entries discarded first when limit exceeded.
- **FR-008**: System MUST provide non-blocking user feedback when undo or redo is not available.
- **FR-009**: System MUST preserve data integrity—undo/redo must never produce duplicate IDs or orphaned nodes.
- **FR-010**: System MUST reset history when a different file/outline is loaded to prevent cross-contamination.
- **FR-011**: *(MVP Scope Note)* System treats each user-initiated operation as one atomic history step. Multi-node move operations (e.g., moving a contiguous selection) are deferred to future enhancements; current implementation supports single-node operations only.
- **FR-012**: System MUST ensure performance of undo or redo feels immediate (see Success Criteria).
- **FR-013**: System MUST allow sequential chaining of at least 50 undo operations without degradation.
- **FR-014**: System MUST exclude purely derived status indicators (e.g., autosave timestamp, mode indicators) from history state.
- **FR-015**: System MUST include fold/unfold and zoom operations in undo/redo history to restore complete view state.
- **FR-016**: System MUST discard history on application restart or outline reload (no cross-session persistence).
- **FR-017**: System MUST ensure all nodes affected by an undo/redo operation are visible to the user by scrolling to the primary changed node and restoring focus appropriately, preventing changes from occurring outside the current viewport without user awareness. Focus restoration follows these rules:
  
  - **Forward operations** (normal mutations):
    - **Single-node edit** (text change): The edited node itself
    - **Node creation** (insert): The newly created node
    - **Node deletion**: The parent node of the deleted node (or next sibling if parent is root)
    - **Node move** (up/down): The moved node itself
    - **Indent/dedent**: The indented/dedented node itself
    - **Fold/unfold**: The node whose fold state changed
    - **Zoom in/out**: The node that became the new zoom root (zoom in) or the previous zoom root's parent (zoom out)
  
  - **Undo operations** (reversing mutations):
    - **General principle**: Restore focus to where it was BEFORE the operation was performed, capturing the user's actual focus at operation time (not the previous operation's result)
    - **Exception - DELETE undo**: Focus on the restored node (the node that was deleted), not where focus moved after deletion
    - **Exception - INITIAL undo**: Focus on the node that was focused when the application started (typically the first child of root). **MUST NOT** be null or root as this would lock user out of keyboard navigation (critical bug if occurs).
    - **Rationale**: Undo reverses both the tree mutation AND the focus change, returning the user to their previous context
    - **Navigation between operations**: When user navigates between operations without triggering new mutations, the focus-before-operation captures this navigation, preserving user's actual working context
    - **CRITICAL**: Focus MUST NEVER be null or root after any operation (including undo to initial state). Root is not visible/navigable in the outline UI. System must always maintain a valid focusable node (child of root) to prevent UI lockout.
  
  - **Redo operations** (replaying mutations):
    - **General principle**: Restore focus to where it would be if performing the operation forward (same as forward operation)
    - **Rationale**: Redo replays the original operation including its focus change
  
  - **Concrete Examples**:
    
    **Example 1 - Simple undo/redo (no navigation between)**:
    ```
    Initial state: Focus on node-A
    1. Edit node-A text → Focus stays on node-A
    2. Undo → Focus returns to node-A (before the edit)
    3. Redo → Focus returns to node-A (after the edit)
    ```
    
    **Example 2 - Navigation between operations**:
    ```
    Initial state: Focus on node-A
    1. Edit node-A text → Focus stays on node-A
    2. Navigate to node-B (NO history recording)
    3. Fold node-B → Focus stays on node-B
    4. Undo fold → Focus returns to node-B (where it was before fold, not node-A from previous operation)
    5. Undo edit → Focus returns to node-A (where it was before edit)
    ```
    
    **Example 3 - Delete operation special handling**:
    ```
    Initial state: Focus on node-X
    1. Delete node-X → Focus moves to parent node-P
    2. Undo delete → Focus returns to node-X (the restored node), NOT to node-P
    3. Redo delete → Focus moves to node-P (replaying the delete operation)
    ```
    
    **Example 4 - Multiple operations with navigation**:
    ```
    Initial state: Focus on node-A
    1. Edit node-A → Focus: node-A
    2. Navigate to node-B (no recording)
    3. Insert child under node-B → Focus: new-child-1
    4. Navigate to node-C (no recording)
    5. Delete node-C → Focus: parent-of-C
    6. Undo delete → Focus: node-C (restored)
    7. Undo insert → Focus: node-B (where focus was before insert, not node-A)
    8. Undo edit → Focus: node-A (where focus was before edit)
    9. Redo edit → Focus: node-A (after edit)
    10. Redo insert → Focus: new-child-1 (after insert)
    11. Redo delete → Focus: parent-of-C (after delete)
    ```
    
    **Example 5 - Undo to initial state**:
    ```
    Initial state: Focus on first child (e.g., "welcome-1" on app startup)
    1. Insert node-A → Focus: node-A
    2. Navigate back to welcome-1 (no recording)
    3. Insert node-B → Focus: node-B
    4. Undo insert node-B → Focus: welcome-1 (where focus was before insert)
    5. Undo insert node-A → Focus: welcome-1 (where focus was at initial state)
    
    Note: Current implementation may return null or root focus on undo to INITIAL state, 
    which is a BUG (locks user out). Root is not visible/navigable. This will be fixed 
    during refactoring to ensure focus is always on a valid child node.
    ```
- **FR-018** *(Optional for MVP)*: System SHOULD provide minimal but clear visual feedback during undo/redo operations to indicate which nodes have changed, allowing users to immediately perceive what was affected (e.g., highlight, flash, or temporary color change on modified nodes and their ancestors). May be deferred to post-MVP polish.
- **FR-019** *(Optional for MVP)*: If visual feedback is implemented, system MUST ensure it is non-intrusive and does not disrupt keyboard navigation or subsequent operations; it should fade or clear automatically after operation completion.
- **FR-020**: System MUST NEVER allow focus to be null, undefined, or root after any operation, including undo to initial state. Both null focus and root focus create a critical bug where the user is locked out of keyboard navigation (root is not visible/navigable in the outline UI). If the historical focus state is unavailable or invalid (e.g., node was deleted), the system MUST select a reasonable default focus on a child node of root (e.g., first child). The application must ensure there is always at least one child node of root available for focus.

### Key Entities

- **Change Record**: Represents one reversible user mutation; stores complete tree state before and after the operation (full OutlineNode tree). Includes operation type for debugging. Simple snapshot approach eliminates complexity of per-node tracking while enabling trivial restoration logic.
- **History Buffer**: Ordered collection of Change Records with pointer index (current state boundary) enabling traversal backward (undo) and forward (redo). Ring buffer with configurable capacity (default 50 steps).

## Success Criteria *(mandatory)*

### Measurable Outcomes

- **SC-001**: Users can undo any of the last 50 qualifying edits with 100% accuracy (structure and content restored) during testing.
- **SC-002**: At least 95% of undo or redo operations complete within 100ms (perceived as instant) for outlines up to 1000 nodes. Note: Initial keyboard response must remain <16ms per Constitution Principle III; the 100ms target applies to complete operation including state restoration and rendering.
- **SC-003**: No data corruption incidents (duplicate IDs, orphaned nodes) detected in automated stress test of 10,000 mixed operations.
- **SC-004**: Redo correctly reapplies ≥ 99% of valid undone steps in automated verification (excluding cases intentionally invalidated by new edits).
- **SC-005**: History memory footprint remains within acceptable qualitative limit (users do not experience slowdowns) while retaining at least 50 steps.
- **SC-006**: 100% of undo/redo actions affecting previously hidden nodes result in those nodes being visible immediately after the operation without manual user intervention.
- **SC-007** *(Optional for MVP)*: Visual feedback on changed nodes is displayed for all undo/redo operations (if FR-018 implemented).
- **SC-008**: Scroll repositioning to reveal changed nodes does not interrupt keyboard navigation flow or cause jarring visual jumps lasting longer than 200ms.

## Scope & Boundaries

In Scope: Structural edits (create, delete, move, indent/dedent), committed text changes (navigation mode only), view operations (fold/unfold, zoom). Redo functionality. History capacity enforcement. Feedback when unavailable. Single-node operations (multi-node atomic operations deferred to future).

Clarifications Resolved: 
- Fold/unfold and zoom operations **included** in history (trivial with full-tree approach)
- Text editing tracked at commit-level only (navigation mode); keystroke-level undo delegated to future OpenTUI textarea component
- Default capacity reduced to 50 steps (sufficient for casual usage)
- Undo/redo history not persisted across sessions or restarts
- Keyboard shortcuts: dual-mode support (standard Ctrl+Z/Ctrl+Shift+Z and vim-style u/Ctrl+r)
- Visual feedback (node highlighting) optional for MVP, may be deferred to post-MVP polish

Out of Scope (for this feature iteration): Multi-file unified history, cross-document undo, persistent history storage, multi-user collaborative merges, granular selection undo, multi-node atomic move operations, keystroke-level text editing undo (future textarea component).

## Assumptions

1. Session-based history suffices for initial release (reset on outline load/restart).
2. History capacity of 50 steps meets user expectations for casual usage; can be tuned later if needed.
3. Full-tree storage is memory-efficient for typical outline sizes (< 1000 nodes) and dramatically simplifies implementation.
4. View state (fold/zoom) is integral to user experience and should be undoable.
5. Autosave boundaries do not truncate or alter history order.
6. Keyboard shortcuts are dual-mode: standard (Ctrl+Z/Ctrl+Shift+Z) for broad accessibility, vim-style (u/Ctrl+r) for keyboard-first power users.
7. Text editing undo at keystroke-level will be handled by future OpenTUI textarea upgrade; current implementation handles structure-level changes (node creation/deletion/moves/commits). Both systems coexist without conflict as they operate at different granularities.
8. Navigation-level history persists across mode switches, allowing users to undo structural changes (e.g., node creation/deletion via Enter/Backspace in edit mode) after returning to navigation mode.
9. Performance validation uses existing benchmarking utilities (performance-monitor.ts) with 100ms threshold for undo/redo operations.

## Dependencies

- Existing tree operation integrity (create/move/delete) must remain atomic.
- ID generation uniqueness (already established) ensures restoration fidelity.
- Validation routines must accept reverted states without false corruption flags.

## Risks & Mitigations

- Risk: Large history may impact performance. Mitigation: Bounded buffer (50 steps); validate undo/redo operations meet 100ms threshold using existing benchmarking utilities.
- Risk: Ambiguous scope of UI state history could confuse users. Mitigation: Clarify and either include consistently or exclude entirely.
- Risk: Undo of complex chained operations (future multi-select) may need refinement. Mitigation: Treat as atomic by design now.

## Validation Readiness

All clarification markers resolved; specification contains no [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] items. Ready for planning phase.

content.content:
1	# Feature Specification: Multiline and Read-Only Node Support
2	
3	**Feature Branch**: `003-multiline-readonly-nodes`
4	**Created**: 2025-11-10
5	**Status**: Draft
6	**Input**: User description: "specify the replacement for the nodes to be either multiline editable or readonly. Research the latest OpenTUI code to use it's text area component for multiline editing. I want flexibility in the code to be able to load a read-only outline (or any tree-like structure, like a filesystem directory tree), or have a fully-editable interactivity for note taking and such."
7	
8	## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*
9	
10	### User Story 1 - View Read-Only Outline (Priority: P1)
11	
12	Users need to browse and navigate through read-only tree structures (like filesystem directories, documentation hierarchies, or reference materials) without accidentally modifying content.
13	
14	**Why this priority**: This is the foundation for using the outliner as a navigation and reference tool. It enables viewing existing hierarchical data without risk of accidental edits, which is essential for browsing codebases, file systems, or other immutable content.
15	
16	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading a read-only outline file and verifying that navigation works but editing operations are disabled or have no effect. Delivers immediate value for users who need to browse hierarchical information.
17	
18	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
19	
20	1. **Given** a read-only outline file is loaded, **When** the user navigates through nodes using arrow keys, **Then** the outline displays correctly and focus moves between nodes
21	2. **Given** a node is selected in read-only mode, **When** the user attempts to press edit keys (i, a, Enter), **Then** the edit mode is not activated or displays a "read-only" indicator
22	3. **Given** a read-only outline is displayed, **When** the user attempts structural operations (indent, dedent, move, delete), **Then** the operations are prevented and a status message indicates the outline is read-only
23	4. **Given** a read-only outline, **When** the user uses fold/unfold and zoom operations, **Then** these view-only operations work normally
24	5. **Given** the user launches the app with `--read-only` flag, **When** any outline file is loaded, **Then** the outline is in read-only mode for this session
25	6. **Given** a file opened with `--read-only` flag, **When** the file is saved, **Then** the file format remains unchanged (no read-only flag is persisted)
26	
27	---
28	
29	### User Story 2 - Edit Notes (Priority: P2)
30	
31	Users need to create and edit outline nodes with flexible text content - from simple single-line entries for task lists to longer multiline content for meeting notes, code snippets, or explanations.
32	
33	**Why this priority**: This provides the core editing functionality that makes the outliner useful for both simple and complex note-taking. Using multiline editing as the default covers both use cases with predictable UX (following the constitution's simplicity principle).
34	
35	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by creating nodes with various content (single-line and multiline), verifying that text wraps correctly, editing preserves content, and all outline operations work. Delivers value for all editing scenarios through a single, consistent interface.
36	
37	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
38	
39	1. **Given** an editable outline, **When** the user activates edit mode on a node, **Then** a multiline text area is displayed
40	2. **Given** a node is in edit mode, **When** the user types text without pressing Shift+Enter, **Then** the text is displayed as a single line
41	3. **Given** a node is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Shift+Enter, **Then** a line break is inserted within the node
42	4. **Given** a node with children is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Enter, **Then** edit mode exits and a new first child node is created
43	5. **Given** a node without children is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Enter, **Then** edit mode exits and a new sibling node is created below
44	6. **Given** an empty node is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Enter, **Then** nothing happens (prevents empty node creation)
45	7. **Given** a node has text (single or multiline), **When** the user presses Escape, **Then** edit mode exits and the node displays in navigation mode
46	8. **Given** an empty node is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Escape, **Then** the empty node is deleted and focus moves to the previous node
47	9. **Given** a node with content, **When** the user performs structural operations (indent, dedent, move), **Then** the operations complete successfully and preserve all text content
48	10. **Given** a multiline node is displayed, **When** the user navigates to other nodes, **Then** the multiline text remains visible and does not interfere with navigation
49	11. **Given** a node with children is being deleted, **When** the delete operation executes, **Then** the children are promoted (moved to previous sibling as children, or to parent as siblings) and the node is removed
50	12. **Given** an empty node is focused, **When** the user presses 'o' or 'O', **Then** nothing happens (prevents empty node creation)
51	13. **Given** a node is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Tab, **Then** the node is indented and edit mode is preserved
52	14. **Given** a node is in edit mode, **When** the user presses Shift+Tab, **Then** the node is dedented and edit mode is preserved
53	
54	---
55	
56	### Edge Cases
57	
58	- What happens when a multiline node contains many lines (e.g., 50+ lines) and terminal height is limited?
59	- What happens when loading a read-only outline that was previously editable?
60	- How does the system handle pasting multiline text into a node?
61	- How does fold/unfold interact with multiline nodes that are tall?
62	- What happens when zoom focuses on a multiline node that exceeds viewport height?
63	- How does the system handle very long lines (200+ characters) that need wrapping?
64	- What happens when deleting a node with children when there's no previous sibling and no parent to promote to?
65	- What happens when the user presses Esc on an empty node that is the only node in the outline?
66	- What happens when pressing 'o'/'O' on an empty node that has children?
67	
68	## Requirements *(mandatory)*
69	
70	### Functional Requirements
71	
72	- **FR-001**: System MUST support a read-only mode where navigation works but editing operations are disabled
73	- **FR-002**: System MUST indicate visually when an outline is in read-only mode (e.g., status bar message, different styling)
74	- **FR-003**: System MUST prevent all editing operations (text changes, node creation/deletion, structural moves) in read-only mode
75	- **FR-004**: System MUST allow view-only operations (fold/unfold, zoom, navigation) in read-only mode
76	- **FR-005**: System MUST provide a multiline text input component that supports line breaks and text wrapping
77	- **FR-006**: System MUST wrap multiline text appropriately within the terminal width

81	- **FR-010**: In edit mode on a node with children, pressing Enter MUST exit edit mode and create a new first child node (empty, with cursor at end)
82	- **FR-010a**: In edit mode on a node without children, pressing Enter MUST exit edit mode and create a new sibling node below (empty, with cursor at end)
83	- **FR-010b**: In edit mode on an empty node, pressing Enter MUST do nothing (prevents empty node creation)
84	- **FR-011**: In edit mode, pressing Shift+Enter or Alt+Enter MUST insert a line break within the current node
85	- **FR-012**: System MUST handle copy/paste of multiline text appropriately

87	- **FR-014**: When in navigation mode, pressing Enter MUST activate edit mode on the current node with cursor at the end of text
88	- **FR-015**: When in navigation mode, pressing 'a' MUST activate edit mode on the current node with cursor at the end of text
89	- **FR-016**: When in navigation mode, pressing 'i' MUST activate edit mode on the current node with cursor at the beginning of text

91	- **FR-018**: When pressing Backspace in navigation mode on an empty node, the node MUST be deleted and focus moved to the end of the previous node's text

93	- **FR-020**: When deleting a node with children, the children MUST be promoted (moved to previous sibling as children, or to parent as siblings if no previous sibling exists)

96	- **FR-023**: The 'o' key MUST create a first child (prepend) if the current node has children; otherwise create a sibling after the current node. The 'O' key MUST always create a sibling before the current node

98	- **FR-024**: In edit mode, Tab key MUST indent the current node and preserve edit mode

118	- **SC-004**: Nodes with up to 20 lines of text display and scroll correctly in the terminal viewport
119	- **SC-005**: Read-only mode is clearly indicated in the UI (status bar or title) and users receive feedback when attempting disabled operations
120	- **SC-006**: File format supports multiline text with newlines preserved across save/load cycles (read-only is session-state only)
121	- **SC-007**: Navigation performance remains acceptable (no visible lag) with outlines containing up to 50 multiline nodes
